title: Conditional rendering

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Conditional rendering; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to Conditional rendering
sources.claim: Conditional rendering selects node descriptions with if, ternary, logical expressions, variables, or early returns.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Conditional rendering selects node descriptions with if, ternary, logical expressions, variables, or early returns.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A count check uses count > 0 && so zero does not appear as stray content. Your goal is to implement and test complete conditional UI states. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Declarative UI and re-rendering; html semantics; JSX expressions and attributes; javascript control selection. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Conditional rendering selects node descriptions with if, ternary, logical expressions, variables, or early returns. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Falsy values such as zero can render unexpectedly with &&, and branch changes can preserve or reset state by identity. This draft therefore rejects: value && <Panel/> always renders nothing for every falsy value without displaying that value.

includes: Render explicit loading, empty, success and conditional branches without leaking falsey values accidentally.
Implement and test complete conditional UI states.
excludes: Falsy values such as zero can render unexpectedly with &&, and branch changes can preserve or reset state by identity.

outcomes: Implement and test complete conditional UI states.

examples.title: Conditional rendering: concrete case
examples.situation: A count check uses count > 0 && so zero does not appear as stray content.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A count check uses count > 0 && so zero does not appear as stray content.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Logical AND can render zero; use an explicit Boolean condition when the left value is displayable.
examples.result: The result follows because Logical AND can render zero; use an explicit Boolean condition when the left value is displayable.

examples.title: Conditional rendering: boundary case
examples.situation: Falsy values such as zero can render unexpectedly with &&, and branch changes can preserve or reset state by identity.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Falsy values such as zero can render unexpectedly with &&, and branch changes can preserve or reset state by identity.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: value && <Panel/> always renders nothing for every falsy value without displaying that value.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Logical AND can render zero; use an explicit Boolean condition when the left value is displayable.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: value && <Panel/> always renders nothing for every falsy value without displaying that value.
misconceptions.correction: Logical AND can render zero; use an explicit Boolean condition when the left value is displayable.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete React: Component-Based Browser Applications artifact to explain why this belief fails: value && <Panel/> always renders nothing for every falsy value without displaying that value.

analogy.example: A state-driven component workshop can model Conditional rendering, where inputs and state produce a UI description.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Conditional rendering before using exact platform terms
limitations: A workshop does not reproduce reconciliation identity, hook ordering, concurrent rendering, effects, or browser accessibility APIs.
